content.full_text:
Memo to the File

Re: Telephone Conversation with Bob Girard of 2/23/76

In a telephone conversation with Bob Girard of

Stanford Univ., we discussed Stanford's intention to

spend approximately $10,000 to print information re-

lative to the nuclear initiative. The information

would include analysis of the provisions of the

initiative, a legal analysis, a statement by the

proponents and a statement by the opponents. Bob G.

indicated that the program would be impartial in the

sense that Stanford would not expressly advocate the

passage or defeat of the initiative but would merely

try to provide all points of view and, thus, provide

a public service by supplying the public with more

information relative to this important issue. In

addition, he indicated that any pamphlet produced

probably would contain a disclaimer to the effect

that Stanford Univ. does not advocate the passage

or defeat of the measure.

I inquired about why the proponents apparently

are in favor of such a program and whether the proponents

had any involvement in it. He responded that the only reason

they favor the program is that they feel their case is stronger

on the merits and that any exposure of both sides of the issue

therefore, works in their favor. He also stated that the

proponents will not participate in the program other than

to provide a statement of their reasons relative to why the

initiative ought to pass.

- 2 -

I informed him that under these circumstances, I
did not believe that Stanford Univ. would become a
"committee" by reason of the expenditure of funds. We
discussed the Buckley decision, and I indicated that I
did not think it could be interpreted to require dis-
closure by an entity which was issue-oriented and
merely presented both sides of an issue without espressly
advocating one side or the other.




K. Finney

/pvp

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Stanford University's $10,000 expenditure to print impartial information about a nuclear initiative, presenting analysis and statements from both proponents and opponents without express advocacy, would require the university to register as a 'committee' under campaign finance law.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC staff advised that Stanford University would not become a 'committee' by reason of the expenditure because the entity was issue-oriented, presented both sides without expressly advocating one position, and such disclosure would not be required under the Buckley decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal memo documenting phone conversation rather than formal advice letter; no traditional QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; staff conclusion appears in body text; Buckley decision referenced as relevant precedent; document initialed by K. Finney

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Stanford University's $10,000 expenditure to print impartial information about a nuclear initiative, presenting analysis and statements from both proponents and opponents without express advocacy, would require the university to register as a 'committee' under campaign finance law.

CONCLUSION: FPPC staff advised that Stanford University would not become a 'committee' by reason of the expenditure because the entity was issue-oriented, presented both sides without expressly advocating one position, and such disclosure would not be required under the Buckley decision.
embedding.first_500_words: Memo to the File Re: Telephone Conversation with Bob Girard of 2/23/76 In a telephone conversation with Bob Girard of Stanford Univ., we discussed Stanford's intention to spend approximately $10,000 to print information re- lative to the nuclear initiative. The information would include analysis of the provisions of the initiative, a legal analysis, a statement by the proponents and a statement by the opponents. Bob G. indicated that the program would be impartial in the sense that Stanford would not expressly advocate the passage or defeat of the initiative but would merely try to provide all points of view and, thus, provide a public service by supplying the public with more information relative to this important issue. In addition, he indicated that any pamphlet produced probably would contain a disclaimer to the effect that Stanford Univ. does not advocate the passage or defeat of the measure. I inquired about why the proponents apparently are in favor of such a program and whether the proponents had any involvement in it. He responded that the only reason they favor the program is that they feel their case is stronger on the merits and that any exposure of both sides of the issue therefore, works in their favor. He also stated that the proponents will not participate in the program other than to provide a statement of their reasons relative to why the initiative ought to pass. - 2 - I informed him that under these circumstances, I did not believe that Stanford Univ. would become a "committee" by reason of the expenditure of funds. We discussed the Buckley decision, and I indicated that I did not think it could be interpreted to require dis- closure by an entity which was issue-oriented and merely presented both sides of an issue without espressly advocating one side or the other. K. Finney /pvp
embedding.summary: FPPC staff memo documenting a telephone conversation with Stanford University regarding their proposed $10,000 expenditure to print impartial information about a nuclear initiative. Staff concluded that Stanford would not become a 'committee' requiring registration and that disclosure would not be required.
